items.title: Build the General Ledger report as a deterministic, company-scoped query
items.description: The General Ledger report lists GL entries for an account and date range, in posting order with a running balance, derived directly from the immutable GLEntry rows and scoped to the user's company. It is a deterministic query: the same inputs always yield the same output because it reads committed ledger rows, not mutable state.
items.applicability: The core drill-down report of any generated ERP accounting module.
items.example: GeneralLedger(account: Bank, from: 01-Jun, to: 30-Jun, company: A) returns each voucher line with a running balance, filtered to CompanyId == A.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: order deterministically (posting date then a stable tiebreaker) so the running balance is reproducible. Test implication: assert the opening plus movements equals the closing balance and that company scope is applied. The report reflects posted GL entries only; unposted or draft documents do not appear, which is correct but must be understood by the reader.

items.title: Build the Trial Balance as a deterministic, company-scoped query
items.description: The Trial Balance aggregates debit and credit totals per account as of a date, scoped to company, and must net to zero across all accounts. It is computed deterministically from GL entries and is the integrity check that the ledger is balanced before producing the financial statements.
items.applicability: The period-close integrity report of a generated ERP accounting module.
items.example: TrialBalance(asOf: 30-Jun, company: A) lists each account's debit and credit totals; the grand total debits equals total credits.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: sum from GL entries with explicit precision so rounding does not break the zero-net check. Test implication: assert total debits equal total credits for a populated company. A balanced trial balance proves arithmetic balance, not that every entry was posted to the correct account.

items.title: Build Profit-and-Loss and Balance Sheet from account classification
items.description: The Profit-and-Loss report sums Income and Expense accounts for a period, and the Balance Sheet presents Asset, Liability and Equity balances as of a date with net profit flowing into equity. Both are deterministic, company-scoped queries derived from the same GL the trial balance uses, so the three reports reconcile.
items.applicability: The primary financial statements of a generated ERP accounting module.
items.example: ProfitAndLoss(period, company: A) nets income minus expense to a profit figure; BalanceSheet(asOf, company: A) shows assets equal to liabilities plus equity including that profit.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: classify accounts by RootType and derive net profit consistently so the balance sheet balances. Test implication: assert assets equal liabilities plus equity and that P&L profit matches the equity movement. The statements present the implemented classification; they do not assert conformance to a specific reporting standard's format or disclosures.

items.title: Build AR and AP aging as deterministic, company-scoped queries
items.description: AR and AP aging reports bucket outstanding receivables and payables by age band (for example current, 30, 60, 90+ days) per party, scoped to company, computed deterministically from open invoice balances and their due dates. They drive collections and payment planning.
items.applicability: Receivables and payables management in a generated ERP with subledger control accounts.
items.example: ARAging(asOf, company: A) lists each customer's outstanding balance split into age buckets relative to invoice due dates.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute aging from due date and the as-of date consistently, netting payments against the right invoices. Test implication: assert an invoice moves buckets as the as-of date crosses its due-date thresholds. Aging reflects recorded open items; disputed amounts or unapplied credits need correct application first or the buckets mislead.

items.title: Build Stock Ledger and Stock Balance as deterministic, company-scoped queries
items.description: The Stock Ledger lists every stock movement for an item and warehouse with a running quantity and value, and the Stock Balance summarises current quantity and valuation per item and warehouse. Both are deterministic, company-scoped queries derived from posted stock entries, mirroring the GL approach for inventory.
items.applicability: Inventory reporting in a generated ERP with a stock module.
items.example: StockLedger(item, warehouse, period, company: A) shows each movement with running quantity; StockBalance(asOf, company: A) shows on-hand quantity and value per item and warehouse.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive running quantity and value from the same posting source the movements wrote so the ledger and balance agree. Test implication: assert the stock balance equals the stock ledger's closing running figure. The reports reflect the implemented valuation method; switching methods changes valuation and is out of scope of the report itself.

items.title: Build Sales and Purchase summary reports as deterministic, company-scoped queries
items.description: Sales and Purchase summary reports aggregate invoiced sales and purchases over a period by dimension (customer, supplier, item or period), scoped to company, computed deterministically from posted documents. They give management a roll-up view without ad-hoc querying.
items.applicability: Management reporting in a generated ERP with selling and buying modules.
items.example: SalesSummary(period, groupBy: customer, company: A) totals invoiced sales per customer for the period from posted sales invoices.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: aggregate from posted documents with explicit grouping so totals are reproducible. Test implication: assert the summary total matches the sum of the underlying posted documents. Summaries reflect posted invoices only; quotations, orders and drafts are excluded by design and must not be read as committed revenue.

items.title: Import CSV with per-row validation and rejected-row capture
items.description: Bulk CSV import must validate each row independently and import the valid rows while capturing the invalid ones with their errors, rather than aborting the whole file on the first bad row. Partial success with a clear rejection report is far more usable for correcting and re-importing data.
items.applicability: Bulk data loading of master data or opening balances into a generated ERP.
items.example: Importing 1000 customer rows, 12 with a missing required field, imports 988 and reports the 12 rejected rows with their row numbers and validation messages for correction.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate row by row and collect errors instead of throwing on first failure; wrap accepted rows so a later failure does not leave a half-imported row. Test implication: import a file with mixed valid and invalid rows and assert the valid ones persist and the invalid ones are reported. Per-row import accepts partial success, so the caller must reconcile the rejected rows; a file is not 'imported' until those are resolved.

items.title: Track imports with an ImportBatch carrying row counts and errors
items.description: Each import run is recorded as an ImportBatch capturing TotalRows, ImportedRows, FailedRows and the collected Errors, giving a durable, queryable record of what was loaded and what was rejected. This turns an import from a fire-and-forget action into an auditable, reviewable operation.
items.applicability: Every bulk import into a generated ERP that needs traceability.
items.example: An ImportBatch row records {TotalRows: 1000, ImportedRows: 988, FailedRows: 12, Errors: [...]}; an operator opens the batch later to see exactly which rows failed and why.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: persist the batch and its per-row errors in the same transaction as the imported rows so counts cannot disagree with reality. Test implication: assert ImportedRows + FailedRows equals TotalRows and that the errors match the failures. The batch records the outcome; it does not automatically re-import corrected rows, which is a follow-up action.

items.title: Record import and export as audited, scope-respecting actions
items.description: Imports and exports are security-relevant data movements and must be recorded as audit actions with actor, time and scope, and must respect the user's role and company scope: an export returns only data the user is permitted to see, and an import is attributed to the importing user. Bulk data movement cannot bypass the controls that govern interactive access.
items.applicability: Import and export features of a generated ERP with RBAC and company scoping.
items.example: A Company A user exports the trial balance; the audit records {action: export, actor, company: A, report: trial-balance} and the file contains only Company A data.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: apply the same company and role filters to bulk paths as to interactive ones, and write an audit row per import/export. Test implication: assert an export excludes other companies' data and that the action is audited. The audit captures that an export occurred and its scope; it cannot retract data once a permitted user has exported it, so export permissions must be granted carefully.

items.title: Treat reports as query-based with partial export coverage
items.description: The reporting layer is a set of deterministic, scope-respecting queries. It deliberately does not include a business-intelligence engine or a print/report designer, and export coverage is partial — not every report or screen has an export. This boundary is stated honestly so users do not assume full BI or universal export.
items.applicability: Setting expectations about the reporting and export capability of a generated ERP.
items.example: Documentation states 'reports are query-based; there is no BI cube or print-designer, and export is available on a subset of reports', so a stakeholder plans external tooling for advanced analytics.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep reports as plain queries and clearly mark which ones offer export rather than implying universal coverage. Test implication: assert the reports that claim export actually produce a correctly scoped file. The honesty boundary means advanced visualisation, pivoting, scheduled distribution and pixel-perfect print layouts are out of scope and require additional tooling the generated baseline does not provide.
